cases.text: The early morning sun cast long shadows across the empty street.

cases.text: She had been waiting for nearly an hour. The bus, as usual, was late. A cold wind picked up, rustling the leaves at her feet.

cases.text: In the quiet town of Millbrook, news traveled faster than the wind. By the time the sheriff arrived at the scene, half the residents had already gathered, whispering theories and trading half-remembered facts. Nobody really knew what had happened, but everybody had an opinion.

cases.text: The expedition departed on March 14th, 1923, with twelve men, three sleds, and provisions enough for sixty days. Within two weeks, a sudden blizzard had separated the lead party from the supply train; what should have been a routine crossing became a desperate fight for survival. By the time rescue arrived, only four members of the original team remained, their journals later hailed as a remarkable record of polar exploration.

cases.text: The history of human flight is, in many respects, a history of stubborn refusal. For thousands of years, observers watched birds soar effortlessly above them and concluded that humans were not meant to join them. Yet inventors persisted. They built wings of feathers and wax; gliders of silk and bamboo; balloons filled with hydrogen, helium, or simply hot air from a smoky fire. Each failure taught something new about lift, drag, and the curious behavior of fluids over these curved surfaces.
